6: Game AI agents enhance player engagement by dynamically adapting

9: Knowledge Base Learning Agents: Agents can leverage Retrieval Augmented
10: Generation (RAG) to maintain a dynamic knowledge base of problem
11: descriptions and proven solutions (see the Chapter 14). By storing successful
12: strategies and challenges encountered, the agent can reference this data
13: during decision-making, enabling it to adapt to new situations more effectively
14: by applying previously successful patterns or avoiding known pitfalls.
15: Case Study: The Self-Improving Coding Agent
17: The Self-Improving Coding Agent (SICA), developed by Maxime Robeyns, Laurence
18: Aitchison, and Martin Szummer, represents an advancement in agent-based learning,
19: demonstrating the capacity for an agent to modify its own source code. This contrasts
20: with traditional approaches where one agent might train another; SICA acts as both
21: the modifier and the modified entity, iteratively refining its code base to improve
23: SICA's self-improvement operates through an iterative cycle (see Fig.1). Initially, SICA
24: reviews an archive of its past versions and their performance on benchmark tests. It
25: selects the version with the highest performance score, calculated based on a
26: weighted formula considering success, time, and computational cost. This selected
27: version then undertakes the next round of self-modification. It analyzes the archive to
28: identify potential improvements and then directly alters its codebase. The modified
29: agent is subsequently tested against benchmarks, with the results recorded in the
30: archive. This process repeats, facilitating learning directly from past performance.
31: This self-improvement mechanism allows SICA to evolve its capabilities without
